summary: Gruber revisits the Uber scandal involving executives visiting a Seoul escort bar, arguing that the real issue isn't the visit itself but Emil Michael's attempt to get a former colleague to lie about it. He acknowledges the moral repugnance of the establishments while noting the cultural context is irrelevant since the group was entirely American. The core argument is that Michael's cover-up attempt was both ethically wrong and strategically stupid, since it directly caused the whistleblower to go public. Gruber concludes that even if one could justify keeping a morally questionable executive for business reasons, keeping one who acts this stupidly is indefensible.
key_points: The cover-up is worse than the original incident — Emil Michael asking Gabi Holzwarth to lie is the real firing offense
Cultural context doesn't apply here since the Uber group was entirely American, not conducting business with Korean counterparts
Michael's attempt to silence Holzwarth backfired catastrophically, directly causing her to speak publicly
There's a distinction between an executive who is morally reprehensible but competent versus one who is both morally reprehensible and stupid
money_quotes: "It's not the crime, it's the cover-up" is a cliché, but in this case it's true.
Personally, I think these establishments are morally repulsive.
They were a bunch of Americans out amongst themselves, so the cultural norms of South Korea aren't relevant.
The real issue here is Emil Michael asking Gabi Holzwarth to lie about the incident.
That's outrageous. It should be considered a firing offense even if you personally think these "karaoke/escort" joints are A-OK.
In addition to being the wrong thing to do, morally, it was also incredibly stupid.
I can see the argument for keeping a personally reprehensible executive who is doing amazing work. I'm not saying I agree with it.

I can't see the logic in keeping a personally reprehensible executive who would do something as stupid — not just wrong but stupid — as calling a former colleague and telling her to lie to the media if asked.
key_insight: The cover-up attempt was not only morally wrong but strategically idiotic, directly causing the very exposure it sought to prevent and removing any possible defense of the executive responsible.
